question_text: Financial statements of a company are prepared as per:
explanation: Financial statements of a company are prepared as per Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013. Schedule III prescribes the format and contents of the Balance Sheet and Statement of Profit & Loss for companies.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Schedule I of the Companies Act
Schedule II of the Companies Act
Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013

elimination_hints.hint: Schedule I contains the model Memorandum of Association, not financial statement formats.
Schedule II deals with useful life and depreciation rates of assets, not financial statement format.
IAS/Ind AS provides principles, but Schedule III of the Companies Act mandates the specific format.

question_text: The Balance Sheet of a company as per Schedule III is presented in:
explanation: Schedule III requires the Balance Sheet to be presented in a VERTICAL format (Statement form), not the traditional horizontal (T-form) format. Assets are shown on one side and Equity & Liabilities on the other, but in a vertical arrangement.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: T-form was used under the old Companies Act, 1956. Schedule III of 2013 Act mandates vertical format.
Companies cannot choose any format — Schedule III prescribes a mandatory vertical format.
Account form is another name for horizontal/T-form. Schedule III uses the statement (vertical) form.

question_text: The two main headings on the Equity & Liabilities side of the Balance Sheet under Schedule III are:
explanation: Schedule III divides the Equity & Liabilities side into: (I) Shareholders' Funds (Share Capital + Reserves & Surplus), (II) Non-Current Liabilities (Long-term borrowings, deferred tax, etc.), and (III) Current Liabilities. But the two broadest categories are Equity (Shareholders' Funds) and Liabilities.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Shareholders' Funds, Non-Current Liabilities, and Current Liabilities

elimination_hints.hint: Fixed Assets and Current Assets are on the ASSETS side, not the Equity & Liabilities side.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the two sides of the Balance Sheet.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital and Revenue are P&L concepts. Balance Sheet uses Shareholders' Funds and Liabilities.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing P&L terminology with Balance Sheet.

elimination_hints.hint: Secured/Unsecured is a sub-classification. The main headings are broader.

question_text: Under Schedule III, which of the following is classified as a Non-Current Asset?
explanation: Non-current assets are assets held for more than one year or not expected to be converted to cash within 12 months. They include: Property, Plant & Equipment (PPE), Intangible Assets, Long-term Investments, Long-term Loans & Advances. Trade receivables are typically current assets.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Trade receivables are usually collected within the operating cycle — they are current assets.
Cash is the most liquid current asset. It is never non-current.
'Short-term' by definition means current (within 12 months).

question_text: Shareholders' Funds in the Balance Sheet includes:
explanation: Shareholders' Funds = Share Capital + Reserves & Surplus. Share Capital includes equity and preference share capital. Reserves & Surplus includes Capital Reserve, Securities Premium, General Reserve, Surplus (P&L balance), DRR, etc.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Debentures are debt (Non-Current Liabilities), not shareholders' funds.
Trade payables are current liabilities owed to suppliers, not shareholders' funds.
Long-term borrowings are Non-Current Liabilities, not part of shareholders' funds.

question_text: Which of the following appears under 'Reserves and Surplus' in the Balance Sheet?
explanation: Reserves and Surplus includes: Capital Reserve, Capital Redemption Reserve, Securities Premium Reserve, Debenture Redemption Reserve, Revaluation Reserve, General Reserve, and Surplus (balance in Statement of P&L). Trade payables are liabilities, not reserves.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Securities Premium Reserve and General Reserve

elimination_hints.hint: Trade payables are current liabilities, not reserves.
Debentures are long-term borrowings under Non-Current Liabilities.
Bank overdraft is a current liability (short-term borrowing), not a reserve.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying a liability as a reserve.

question_text: Under Schedule III, 'Current Liabilities' includes all of the following EXCEPT:
explanation: Current Liabilities include: Short-term borrowings, Trade payables, Other current liabilities, Short-term provisions. Long-term borrowings (like debentures redeemable after 3 years) are Non-Current Liabilities, NOT current.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Trade payables are amounts owed to suppliers — a classic current liability.
Short-term provisions (e.g., provision for tax) are current liabilities.
Short-term borrowings (bank overdraft, etc.) are current liabilities.

question_text: In the Balance Sheet under Schedule III, 'Property, Plant and Equipment' is shown:
explanation: PPE is shown under Non-Current Assets at its NET value (Cost minus Accumulated Depreciation). The gross block and depreciation details are provided in the Notes to Accounts. Only the net figure appears on the face of the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: At original cost (gross block) on the face of the Balance Sheet
At net value (cost minus accumulated depreciation) on the face of the Balance Sheet

elimination_hints.hint: Gross block details go in the Notes. The Balance Sheet face shows only the NET figure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Showing gross block on face of BS.

elimination_hints.hint: PPE is shown at cost less depreciation, not market value (unless revalued).
PPE is by definition a long-term asset — it goes under Non-Current Assets.

question_text: The Balance Sheet of a company shows the financial position at a particular date.
explanation: TRUE. The Balance Sheet is a 'snapshot' of the company's financial position at a specific point in time (usually 31st March). It shows what the company owns (assets), owes (liabilities), and the shareholders' equity on that date.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Balance Sheet is 'as at' a date (point in time). P&L is 'for' a period. They're different.

question_text: Under Schedule III, Goodwill can be shown in the Balance Sheet only if it is acquired (purchased).
explanation: TRUE. Under Schedule III, goodwill is shown under Non-Current Assets (Intangible Assets) only when it is PURCHASED or acquired through a business combination. Self-generated goodwill cannot be recognized as an asset in the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Self-generated goodwill is internally created and cannot be reliably measured. Only purchased goodwill qualifies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking self-generated goodwill can be shown.

question_text: Calls-in-Arrears is shown as a deduction from Subscribed Capital in the Balance Sheet.
explanation: TRUE. Calls-in-Arrears represents unpaid amounts by shareholders. It is shown as a deduction from Subscribed but not fully paid-up capital. This gives the net paid-up capital figure.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Calls-in-Arrears reduces the paid-up capital. It's shown as a subtraction, not an addition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking it's shown separately as an asset.

question_text: The Balance Sheet of a company under Schedule III has two sides: _______ and _______.
explanation: The two sides of the Balance Sheet under Schedule III are: (I) Equity and Liabilities (sources of funds), and (II) Assets (application of funds). In vertical format, Equity & Liabilities are shown first, followed by Assets.
subject: accountancy

question_text: Assets in the Balance Sheet under Schedule III are classified as _______ Assets and _______ Assets.
explanation: Assets are classified as: Non-Current Assets (held for more than 12 months — PPE, intangibles, long-term investments) and Current Assets (expected to be converted to cash within 12 months — inventory, debtors, cash).
subject: accountancy

question_text:
Assertion (A): Provision for Tax is shown under 'Current Liabilities' in the Balance Sheet.
Reason (R): Tax is usually payable within the current financial year or shortly after.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Provision for tax is a short-term obligation — the tax becomes due within the operating cycle and must be paid to the government. Hence it is classified as a current liability (under Short-term Provisions).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The short-term nature of tax payment is exactly why it's classified as current — R explains A directly.
R is correct — tax is usually payable within or shortly after the financial year.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the timing of tax payments.

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — provision for tax IS shown under current liabilities.

question_text:
Match the following items with their classification in the Balance Sheet:

| Column I (Item) | Column II (Classification) |
|---|---|
| (i) Debentures (long-term) | (p) Current Assets |
| (ii) Inventories | (q) Shareholders' Funds |
| (iii) Securities Premium | (r) Non-Current Liabilities |
| (iv) Trade Receivables | (s) Current Assets |

Choose the correct matching:
explanation:
(i) Debentures (long-term) → Non-Current Liabilities → (r)
(ii) Inventories → Current Assets → (p)
(iii) Securities Premium → Shareholders' Funds (Reserves & Surplus) → (q)
(iv) Trade Receivables → Current Assets → (s) [same as (p)]
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Debentures are liabilities (r), not Shareholders' Funds (q). They are debt, not equity.
Inventories are Current Assets (p), not Non-Current Liabilities (r).
Securities Premium is Shareholders' Funds (q), not Current Assets (p).
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying a reserve as an asset.

question_text: Which of the following items appears under 'Non-Current Liabilities'?
explanation: Non-Current Liabilities include: Long-term Borrowings (debentures, term loans), Deferred Tax Liabilities, Other Long-term Liabilities, Long-term Provisions. Trade payables, bank overdraft, and provision for tax are current liabilities.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Trade payables are typically payable within the operating cycle — they are current liabilities.
Bank overdraft is a short-term borrowing facility — it's a current liability.
Tax provision is payable within the year — it's a current liability (short-term provision).

question_text: Under Schedule III, 'Current Assets' include all EXCEPT:
explanation: Current Assets include: Current Investments, Inventories, Trade Receivables, Cash & Cash Equivalents, Short-term Loans & Advances, Other Current Assets. Long-term Investments (held for more than 12 months) are Non-Current Assets.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Inventories (stock) are classic current assets — held for sale in the ordinary course.
Trade receivables (debtors) are due within the operating cycle — they are current assets.
Cash is the most liquid current asset by definition.

question_text: Notes to Accounts form:
explanation: Notes to Accounts are an INTEGRAL part of the financial statements. They provide detailed breakdowns, accounting policies, and additional information that cannot be shown on the face of the Balance Sheet or P&L. They are not optional — they are mandatory.
subject: accountancy

options.text: An integral part of the financial statements
An optional supplement to financial statements
A separate document unrelated to financial statements
Internal management documents not shared with shareholders

elimination_hints.hint: Notes are mandatory, not optional. Schedule III requires detailed notes for each line item.
Notes are directly linked to line items in the BS and P&L. They are very much related.
Notes are shared with all stakeholders as part of published financial statements.

question_text: Under Schedule III, the Balance Sheet must also show figures of:
explanation: Schedule III requires that the Balance Sheet show figures for the current reporting period AND the previous reporting period (comparative figures). This allows users to compare and assess trends. The heading says 'as at [current date]' and 'as at [previous date]'.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both the current year and the previous year (comparative)
Current year and the next three projected years

elimination_hints.hint: Schedule III mandates comparative figures from the previous year alongside current year.
Financial statements show historical data, not projections. Future years are not included.
The primary focus is the current year. Previous year is shown for comparison, not as the only figure.

question_text: Contingent liabilities are disclosed in the financial statements:
explanation: Contingent liabilities (potential obligations that may or may not arise, like pending lawsuits, guarantees given) are NOT recorded as liabilities in the Balance Sheet. They are disclosed by way of a NOTE below the Balance Sheet. They represent possible but uncertain obligations.
subject: accountancy

options.text: As actual liabilities on the Balance Sheet
By way of a note, not as actual liabilities
They are not disclosed at all

elimination_hints.hint: Contingent liabilities are uncertain — they may not materialise. Only actual/probable liabilities are recorded.
elimination_hints.misconception: Recording uncertain items as actual liabilities.

elimination_hints.hint: Contingent liabilities are not expenses. If they materialise, they become provisions or liabilities.
They MUST be disclosed by note. Non-disclosure would violate accounting standards and the Companies Act.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking contingent liabilities can be hidden.
